source_paper_title: Cardinal-Utility Matching Markets: The Quest for Envy-Freeness, Pareto-Optimality, and Efficient Computability
context:
Section 4 (Conclusion), page 23

Is Nash bargaining the best we can do for one-sided markets or is there a way to compute an \(\alpha\)-envy-free and Pareto-optimal lottery for \(\alpha < 2\) in polynomial time?
title: Polynomial-time computation of an \(\alpha\)-envy-free and Pareto-optimal lottery with \(\alpha<2\) in one-sided cardinal-utility matching
latex:
Given an integer \(n\ge 1\), let \(A=\{1,\dots,n\}\) be a set of agents and \(G=\{1,\dots,n\}\) a set of goods. Each agent \(i\in A\) has a nonnegative rational utility \(u_{ij}\in\mathbb{Q}_{\ge 0}\) for each good \(j\in G\). An allocation is a matrix \(x=(x_{ij})_{i\in A,j\in G}\in\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}^{n\times n}\) satisfying the fractional perfect matching constraints
\[
\sum_{j\in G} x_{ij}=1\ \ \forall i\in A,\qquad \sum_{i\in A} x_{ij}=1\ \ \forall j\in G.
\]
Agent \(i\)'s expected utility under \(x\) is \(U_i(x)=\sum_{j\in G} u_{ij}x_{ij}\).

For a constant \(\alpha\ge 1\), call \(x\) \(\alpha\)-approximately envy-free (\(\alpha\)-EF) if for all agents \(i,i'\in A\),
\[
U_i(x)\ge \frac{1}{\alpha}U_i(x_{i'})\quad\text{where }U_i(x_{i'})=\sum_{j\in G}u_{ij}x_{i'j}.
\]
Call \(x\) Pareto-optimal (PO) if there is no other feasible allocation \(y\) such that \(U_i(y)\ge U_i(x)\) for all \(i\in A\) and \(U_{i^*}(y)>U_{i^*}(x)\) for some agent \(i^*\in A\).

Problem: Determine whether there exists a constant \(\alpha<2\) and a polynomial-time algorithm that, given any input utilities \((u_{ij})\), computes a feasible allocation \(x\) that is simultaneously \(\alpha\)-EF and PO.
open_problem_quote: Is Nash bargaining the best we can do for one-sided markets or is there a way to compute an \(\alpha\)-envy-free and Pareto-optimal lottery for \(\alpha < 2\) in polynomial time?
subject_classification: Computational Social Choice / Fair Division (Matching Markets)
